reviewPolicy.note: Records prepared by an AI agent are not maintainer approvals. Only an explicit human action may change a record to maintainer-approved and add approvedBy/approvedAt.

officialSourceReviews.mappingNote: The FTC staff report discusses interface practices that can distort consumers' understanding or ability to act; FairUX uses it as contextual support for a missing or hard-to-find cancellation path for an active subscription, not as a rule-specific compliance finding.
officialSourceReviews.limitations: FTC staff report provides enforcement and research context; it does not create a standalone legal finding.
officialSourceReviews.sourceLocator: Bringing Dark Patterns to Light, Introduction p. 1 and Background pp. 2-3, difficult cancellation and maze-screen examples.

officialSourceReviews.mappingNote: Current Part 425 addresses disclosures and procedures for prenotification negative option plans; FairUX treats it as contextual support for a missing or hard-to-find cancellation path for an active subscription because the current rule does not cover most modern subscriptions.
officialSourceReviews.limitations: Current Part 425 is limited to prenotification negative option plans and must not be treated as direct support for modern subscriptions, free trials, or cancellation UX outside that scope.
officialSourceReviews.sourceLocator: 16 CFR Part 425.1(a)(1)(iii), 425.1(a)(2)-(3), and FTC 2026 ANPRM pp. 4-5 summary of subscriber cancellation procedures for prenotification plans.

officialSourceReviews.mappingNote: The vacated 2024 final rule record explains FTC reasoning about negative-option cancellation paths that are missing or hard to find; FairUX retains it only as historical agency rationale, not current regulatory authority.
officialSourceReviews.limitations: Vacated source is retained only as historical FTC rationale and must not be used as current rule text.
officialSourceReviews.sourceLocator: Federal Register Negative Option Rule final amendments, Supplementary Information B.1 and former § 425.6 Simple cancellation.

officialSourceReviews.mappingNote: The 2026 ANPRM asks whether negative-option requirements should be updated; FairUX uses it as proposed rulemaking context for a missing or hard-to-find cancellation path for an active subscription, not as a current obligation.
officialSourceReviews.limitations: ANPRM is proposed rulemaking and a request for comment, not a final rule, current requirement, or legal verdict.
officialSourceReviews.sourceLocator: Negative Option Rule 2026 ANPRM, Action line and pp. 1-2 overview after vacatur, plus pp. 6-8 existing requirements discussion.

positive.summary: Account settings page signals an active subscription but contains no cancel or manage-subscription path.
positive.testCase: flags an account page that manages a subscription with no cancel path [en]

negative.summary: Active subscription page with a cancel subscription link stays quiet.
negative.testCase: does not flag when a cancel link exists [negative]

uncoveredScenarios.summary: A cancellation path may exist only after authenticated runtime API calls or JavaScript hydration that static HTML cannot observe.
uncoveredScenarios.reason: Prepared review scenario is not backed by an executable corpus test in PR A.
uncoveredScenarios.resolutionCriteria: Add an executable positive, negative, or ambiguous corpus test, or record an explicit maintainer-approved exception during P13 closeout.

locale.en: English subscription and account-management phrases are covered by existing corpus tests.
locale.ja: Japanese active-subscription text is covered, but provider-specific billing vocabulary remains a known review target.

reviewNotes.runtime: Static HTML cannot follow authenticated account flows, post-login navigation, or JavaScript-only cancellation widgets.
reviewNotes.falsePositive: The active-subscription text gate and page-context gate reduce marketing-page false positives.
reviewNotes.evidenceUsefulness: The finding points to the page root because the missing element is an absence signal.
reviewNotes.performance: The rule scans normalized document text and controls once; no network or layout work is required.
reviewNotes.determinism: Results depend only on parsed markup, dictionary matches, and page-context signals.
knownLimitations: Absence of a visible cancel path is not proof that cancellation is impossible.

officialSourceReviews.mappingNote: EDPB consent guidance supports genuine, free choice and the ability to refuse without detriment across EU and EEA contexts; FairUX treats it as contextual because it does not directly prescribe button color, size, contrast, or visual prominence.
officialSourceReviews.limitations: Does not directly regulate visual styling such as contrast, size, or prominence; ICO guidance carries the direct prominence mapping for this rule.
officialSourceReviews.sourceLocator: Guidelines 05/2020, sections 3.1 Free/freely given, 3.1.1 Imbalance of power, and EEA reading note, pp. 3 and 7-10.

officialSourceReviews.mappingNote: ICO consent guidance gives operational expectations for equal refusal, positive opt-in, and granular controls; this directly supports FairUX review of accept and reject consent actions presented with unequal visual prominence in GB consent contexts.
officialSourceReviews.limitations: Consent source supports governance review only and does not determine legal compliance for any individual UI.
officialSourceReviews.sourceLocator: ICO "How do we manage consent in practice?", At a glance bullets and "Our consent mechanism makes it as easy to refuse consent as it is to accept" checklist.

officialSourceReviews.mappingNote: The FTC staff report discusses interface practices that can distort consumers' understanding or ability to act; FairUX uses it as contextual support for accept and reject consent actions presented with unequal visual prominence, not as a rule-specific compliance finding.
officialSourceReviews.limitations: FTC staff report provides enforcement and research context; it does not create a standalone legal finding.
officialSourceReviews.sourceLocator: Bringing Dark Patterns to Light, Section IV printed pp. 15-16 on highlighted preferred choices and greyed rejection, plus Appendix A p. 25 bold Accept/greyed Reject example.

positive.summary: Accept is styled as a primary button while reject is a weaker link when experimental rules are enabled.

negative.summary: The experimental visual-imbalance rule is excluded from default scans.

uncoveredScenarios.summary: Class names may not reflect computed prominence when CSS is external, responsive, or overridden.
uncoveredScenarios.reason: Prepared review scenario is not backed by an executable corpus test in PR A.
uncoveredScenarios.resolutionCriteria: Add an executable positive, negative, or ambiguous corpus test, or record an explicit maintainer-approved exception during P13 closeout.

locale.en: English accept/reject labels are covered by dictionary-driven tests.
locale.ja: Japanese consent labels rely on dictionary coverage and require maintainer review before stable promotion.

reviewNotes.runtime: The rule uses static class and inline-style hints and does not compute actual layout, contrast, or position.
reviewNotes.falsePositive: The rule requires both accept and reject controls plus a large heuristic prominence gap.
reviewNotes.evidenceUsefulness: Evidence includes both controls so reviewers can inspect the comparison directly.
reviewNotes.performance: The rule performs one pass over controls and local style/class parsing.
reviewNotes.determinism: Prominence scoring is deterministic for the parsed markup and dictionary version.
knownLimitations: Computed CSS, viewport position, color contrast, and responsive variants are outside the current static provider.

officialSourceReviews.mappingNote: EDPB consent guidance ties valid consent to freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous choice; this directly supports FairUX review of one consent action bundling multiple processing purposes that should be separable in EU and EEA consent contexts.
officialSourceReviews.limitations: Consent source supports governance review only and does not determine legal compliance for any individual UI.
officialSourceReviews.sourceLocator: Guidelines 05/2020, section 3.1.3 Granularity, paragraphs 42-44, p. 12.

officialSourceReviews.mappingNote: ICO consent guidance gives operational expectations for equal refusal, positive opt-in, and granular controls; this directly supports FairUX review of one consent action bundling multiple processing purposes that should be separable in GB consent contexts.
officialSourceReviews.limitations: Consent source supports governance review only and does not determine legal compliance for any individual UI.
officialSourceReviews.sourceLocator: ICO "How do we manage consent in practice?", "Our consent mechanism includes granular options for different purposes" checklist.

positive.summary: One checkbox bundles terms, privacy policy, and marketing emails.
positive.testCase: flags a checkbox bundling multiple consents [en]

negative.summary: A checkbox for one topic, terms only, stays quiet.
negative.testCase: does not flag a single-topic consent [negative]

uncoveredScenarios.summary: A single checkbox may mention multiple documents without actually requesting multiple independent consents.
uncoveredScenarios.reason: Prepared review scenario is not backed by an executable corpus test in PR A.
uncoveredScenarios.resolutionCriteria: Add an executable positive, negative, or ambiguous corpus test, or record an explicit maintainer-approved exception during P13 closeout.

locale.en: English terms, privacy, marketing, and third-party terms are covered by dictionary groups.
locale.ja: Japanese bundled-consent corpus exists, but legal-document vocabulary should remain reviewable per release.

reviewNotes.runtime: Static label text is sufficient for the current signal; linked policy contents are not fetched.
reviewNotes.falsePositive: The rule requires a checkbox, agreement wording, and two or more configured topics.
reviewNotes.evidenceUsefulness: Evidence is the full checkbox label, which is usually enough for human review.
reviewNotes.performance: The rule is a single pass over checkbox controls with small dictionary lookups.
reviewNotes.determinism: Topic extraction depends only on normalized label text and frozen dictionaries.
knownLimitations: The rule cannot determine whether topics are legally separable in a specific jurisdiction.

officialSourceReviews.mappingNote: Planet49 rejects pre-ticked cookie consent as valid consent; FairUX uses that case-law signal for preselected checkbox controls in a consent context while avoiding a verdict on each implementation.
officialSourceReviews.limitations: Consent source supports governance review only and does not determine legal compliance for any individual UI.
officialSourceReviews.sourceLocator: Planet49 judgment, paragraphs 49-64 and operative part on pre-ticked cookie checkbox consent.

officialSourceReviews.mappingNote: EDPB consent guidance ties valid consent to freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous choice; this directly supports FairUX review of preselected checkbox controls in a consent context in EU and EEA consent contexts.
officialSourceReviews.limitations: Consent source supports governance review only and does not determine legal compliance for any individual UI.
officialSourceReviews.sourceLocator: Guidelines 05/2020, Article 4(11) consent definition discussion and section 3.4 Unambiguous indication of wishes, pp. 18-20.

officialSourceReviews.mappingNote: The FTC staff report discusses interface practices that can distort consumers' understanding or ability to act; FairUX uses it as contextual support for preselected checkbox controls in a consent context, not as a rule-specific compliance finding.
officialSourceReviews.limitations: FTC staff report provides enforcement and research context; it does not create a standalone legal finding.
officialSourceReviews.sourceLocator: Bringing Dark Patterns to Light, Introduction p. 1 pre-checked boxes example and Appendix A printed pp. 24-25 prechecked tracking-cookie checkbox.

positive.summary: Pre-checked marketing checkbox on a consent page is flagged.
positive.testCase: flags a pre-checked marketing box (high) on a consent page [en]

negative.testCase: does not flag an unchecked box [negative]

uncoveredScenarios.summary: A pre-checked non-consent filter can be benign and must remain outside the rule unless context or label signals consent.
uncoveredScenarios.reason: Prepared review scenario is not backed by an executable corpus test in PR A.
uncoveredScenarios.resolutionCriteria: Add an executable positive, negative, or ambiguous corpus test, or record an explicit maintainer-approved exception during P13 closeout.

locale.en: English marketing, terms, privacy, and mild-consent labels drive severity review.
locale.ja: Japanese marketing consent is covered by corpus; untranslated niche consent labels remain a review risk.

reviewNotes.runtime: The rule observes checkbox state from static attributes and cannot observe client-side default changes after hydration.
reviewNotes.falsePositive: Consent-like labels or consent-related page contexts are required before a checked box is flagged.
reviewNotes.evidenceUsefulness: Evidence includes the checkbox locator and label, supporting direct review.
reviewNotes.performance: The rule scans controls once and uses label lookup plus dictionary matching.
reviewNotes.determinism: Severity is derived deterministically from label groups and page contexts.
knownLimitations: A checked attribute may not match runtime state after scripts execute.

officialSourceReviews.mappingNote: EDPB consent guidance ties valid consent to freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous choice; this directly supports FairUX review of a consent prompt that offers acceptance without a comparable rejection path in EU and EEA consent contexts.
officialSourceReviews.limitations: Consent source supports governance review only and does not determine legal compliance for any individual UI.
officialSourceReviews.sourceLocator: Guidelines 05/2020, section 3.1.2 Conditionality, paragraphs 39-41, and section 3.1.3 Granularity, paragraphs 42-44.

officialSourceReviews.mappingNote: ICO consent guidance gives operational expectations for equal refusal, positive opt-in, and granular controls; this directly supports FairUX review of a consent prompt that offers acceptance without a comparable rejection path in GB consent contexts.
officialSourceReviews.limitations: Consent source supports governance review only and does not determine legal compliance for any individual UI.
officialSourceReviews.sourceLocator: ICO "How do we manage consent in practice?", "Our consent mechanism makes it as easy to refuse consent as it is to accept" good/bad practice examples.

officialSourceReviews.mappingNote: The FTC staff report discusses interface practices that can distort consumers' understanding or ability to act; FairUX uses it as contextual support for a consent prompt that offers acceptance without a comparable rejection path, not as a rule-specific compliance finding.
officialSourceReviews.limitations: FTC staff report provides enforcement and research context; it does not create a standalone legal finding.
officialSourceReviews.sourceLocator: Bringing Dark Patterns to Light, Section IV printed pp. 15-16 on highlighted preferred choices, greyed rejection, and interfaces that make rejection unavailable.

positive.summary: Consent banner with accept only is flagged.

negative.summary: Accept and reject controls in the same container stay quiet.
negative.testCase: does not flag when a reject option exists [negative]

uncoveredScenarios.summary: A manage-preferences link may expose refusal one step later; static review records this as a nearby refusal only when dictionary labels match.
uncoveredScenarios.reason: Prepared review scenario is not backed by an executable corpus test in PR A.
uncoveredScenarios.resolutionCriteria: Add an executable positive, negative, or ambiguous corpus test, or record an explicit maintainer-approved exception during P13 closeout.

locale.en: English accept, reject, decline, and manage-preference labels are covered.
locale.ja: Japanese accept-only banners are covered; maintainers should review additional refusal synonyms.

reviewNotes.runtime: The rule does not open preference dialogs or execute consent management platform scripts.
reviewNotes.falsePositive: The rule is scoped to consent and marketing contexts and requires local container absence.
reviewNotes.evidenceUsefulness: Evidence lists up to three accept controls that lack nearby refusal.
reviewNotes.performance: The rule uses local container queries around accept controls.
reviewNotes.determinism: Container matching and dictionary labels are deterministic for a parsed document.
knownLimitations: A refusal option hidden behind runtime-only UI may be missed.

officialSourceReviews.mappingNote: The FTC staff report discusses interface practices that can distort consumers' understanding or ability to act; FairUX uses it as contextual support for checkout-adjacent pricing that lacks fee context before payment, not as a rule-specific compliance finding.
officialSourceReviews.limitations: FTC staff report provides enforcement and research context; it does not create a standalone legal finding.
officialSourceReviews.sourceLocator: Bringing Dark Patterns to Light, Section II Design Elements that Hide or Delay Disclosure of Material Information, pp. 6-9.

officialSourceReviews.mappingNote: The FTC fees FAQ explains total-price and payment-stage disclosure expectations; FairUX uses it to support review of checkout-adjacent pricing that lacks fee context before payment without declaring a fee unfair or deceptive.
officialSourceReviews.limitations: Fee guidance supports disclosure review and does not prove a particular fee is unfair or deceptive.
officialSourceReviews.sourceLocator: FTC Unfair or Deceptive Fees FAQ, "Which mandatory fees or charges must be included" and "When must a business disclose the final amount of payment" sections.

positive.summary: Checkout page shows a price but no nearby tax, shipping, or fee disclosure.
positive.testCase: flags a price on checkout with no fee disclosure [en]

negative.summary: Checkout price with nearby tax and shipping disclosure stays quiet.
negative.testCase: does not flag when fees are disclosed [negative]

uncoveredScenarios.summary: A flow may calculate taxes or shipping after address entry; static checkout HTML cannot determine whether later disclosure is timely.
uncoveredScenarios.reason: Prepared review scenario is not backed by an executable corpus test in PR A.
uncoveredScenarios.resolutionCriteria: Add an executable positive, negative, or ambiguous corpus test, or record an explicit maintainer-approved exception during P13 closeout.

locale.en: English fee, tax, and shipping terms are covered by dictionary groups and checkout context.
locale.ja: Japanese yen prices and checkout copy are covered; shipping/tax synonyms require continued review.

reviewNotes.runtime: The rule cannot compute totals or inspect later checkout steps.
reviewNotes.falsePositive: The rule is narrowed to checkout contexts and local container absence.
reviewNotes.evidenceUsefulness: Evidence identifies the first undisclosed price near checkout.
reviewNotes.performance: The rule uses regex price detection and local container text checks.
reviewNotes.determinism: Price regexes and dictionary fee terms make the result deterministic for static input.
knownLimitations: The FTC fees FAQ has narrower sector scope than the FairUX heuristic; it supports review context only.

officialSourceReviews.mappingNote: The OECD taxonomy describes dark commercial pattern mechanisms that pressure consumer decisions; FairUX uses it as non-jurisdictional context for decline copy that pressures the user through guilt or social discomfort.
officialSourceReviews.limitations: Policy report provides taxonomy context only and is not jurisdiction-specific enforcement authority.
officialSourceReviews.sourceLocator: OECD Dark commercial patterns report, taxonomy discussion of obstruction and forced action in Digital Economy Papers No. 336.

officialSourceReviews.mappingNote: The FTC staff report discusses interface practices that can distort consumers' understanding or ability to act; FairUX uses it as contextual support for decline copy that pressures the user through guilt or social discomfort, not as a rule-specific compliance finding.
officialSourceReviews.limitations: FTC staff report provides enforcement and research context; it does not create a standalone legal finding.
officialSourceReviews.sourceLocator: Bringing Dark Patterns to Light, Background pp. 2-3 and Section I pp. 3-5 on manipulative interface design inducing false beliefs.

positive.summary: Interactive decline option uses guilt-tripping wording.

negative.summary: Non-interactive body copy is not sufficient because the rule requires a control label.
negative.testCase: does not flag guilt-like phrasing in body copy (must be a control) [negative]

uncoveredScenarios.summary: Sarcastic or brand-voice labels may require human judgment beyond dictionary matching.
uncoveredScenarios.reason: Prepared review scenario is not backed by an executable corpus test in PR A.
uncoveredScenarios.resolutionCriteria: Add an executable positive, negative, or ambiguous corpus test, or record an explicit maintainer-approved exception during P13 closeout.

locale.en: English guilt-trip decline phrases are covered by dictionary groups.
locale.ja: Japanese confirmshaming coverage is limited and should remain a dictionary review area.

reviewNotes.runtime: The rule uses accessible/control labels only and does not infer tone from surrounding design.
reviewNotes.falsePositive: Two factors are required: interactive control plus configured shame phrase.
reviewNotes.evidenceUsefulness: Evidence is the exact decline control label.
reviewNotes.performance: The rule is one pass over controls with dictionary matching.
reviewNotes.determinism: Matches are deterministic for normalized labels and dictionaries.
knownLimitations: Tone and cultural interpretation cannot be fully resolved by static text matching.

officialSourceReviews.mappingNote: The FTC staff report discusses interface practices that can distort consumers' understanding or ability to act; FairUX uses it as contextual support for a modal whose close affordance is present but difficult to perceive, not as a rule-specific compliance finding.
officialSourceReviews.limitations: FTC staff report provides enforcement and research context; it does not create a standalone legal finding.
officialSourceReviews.sourceLocator: Bringing Dark Patterns to Light, Background pp. 2-3, difficult cancellation and obstruction examples in modal-like flows.

officialSourceReviews.mappingNote: The WAI-ARIA modal dialog pattern defines expected modal keyboard behavior, including Escape closure; FairUX uses that standard support for a modal whose close affordance is present but difficult to perceive as an interaction-quality signal.
officialSourceReviews.limitations: Accessibility pattern supports modal interaction review but is not a consumer-protection legal authority.
officialSourceReviews.sourceLocator: WAI-ARIA APG Dialog (Modal) Pattern, About This Pattern and Keyboard Interaction, Escape closes dialog.

positive.summary: A modal close button with low opacity is flagged when experimental rules are enabled.

negative.summary: The experimental close-visibility rule is excluded from default scans.

uncoveredScenarios.summary: External CSS, media queries, and contrast calculations may make a close control more or less visible than static hints suggest.
uncoveredScenarios.reason: Prepared review scenario is not backed by an executable corpus test in PR A.
uncoveredScenarios.resolutionCriteria: Add an executable positive, negative, or ambiguous corpus test, or record an explicit maintainer-approved exception during P13 closeout.

locale.en: English close labels and modal class/role hints are covered indirectly through structural helpers.
locale.ja: Japanese close labels rely on accessible-name or class hints; localized icon-only controls remain a review risk.

reviewNotes.runtime: The rule cannot compute actual viewport visibility, contrast, or stacking context.
reviewNotes.falsePositive: The rule requires a modal-like container and a close action with hard-to-see static hints.
reviewNotes.evidenceUsefulness: Evidence identifies the close control, enabling a reviewer to inspect visibility cues.
reviewNotes.performance: The rule scans modal descendants and parses inline style/class hints only.
reviewNotes.determinism: Heuristic thresholds are fixed and independent of runtime layout.
knownLimitations: This remains experimental because static style hints are an incomplete proxy for visual visibility.

officialSourceReviews.mappingNote: The FTC staff report discusses interface practices that can distort consumers' understanding or ability to act; FairUX uses it as contextual support for a modal flow without an observable close action, not as a rule-specific compliance finding.
officialSourceReviews.limitations: FTC staff report provides enforcement and research context; it does not create a standalone legal finding.
officialSourceReviews.sourceLocator: Bringing Dark Patterns to Light, Introduction p. 1 and Background pp. 2-3, maze-screen and hard-to-cancel examples.

officialSourceReviews.mappingNote: The WAI-ARIA modal dialog pattern defines expected modal keyboard behavior, including Escape closure; FairUX uses that standard support for a modal flow without an observable close action as an interaction-quality signal.
officialSourceReviews.limitations: Accessibility pattern supports modal interaction review but is not a consumer-protection legal authority.
officialSourceReviews.sourceLocator: WAI-ARIA APG Dialog (Modal) Pattern, Keyboard Interaction Tab containment and Escape closes dialog requirements.

positive.summary: Modal-like container with no close control is flagged.
positive.testCase: flags a modal with no close control [en]

negative.summary: Modal with aria-label close button stays quiet.
negative.testCase: does not flag a modal with an aria-label close button [negative]

uncoveredScenarios.summary: A modal may close via Escape, outside click, or app state without a visible static close control.
uncoveredScenarios.reason: Prepared review scenario is not backed by an executable corpus test in PR A.
uncoveredScenarios.resolutionCriteria: Add an executable positive, negative, or ambiguous corpus test, or record an explicit maintainer-approved exception during P13 closeout.

locale.en: English visible or accessible close labels are covered by helper dictionaries.
locale.ja: Japanese close labels require accessible-name support or dictionary coverage.

reviewNotes.runtime: The rule cannot execute keyboard handlers, outside-click handlers, or framework state changes.
reviewNotes.falsePositive: Severity is low and the rule only fires for modal-like containers.
reviewNotes.evidenceUsefulness: Evidence points to the modal-like node whose close affordance was absent.
reviewNotes.performance: The rule checks descendants of modal-like nodes only.
reviewNotes.determinism: Modal and close detection use fixed structural and dictionary heuristics.
knownLimitations: Absence of a static close control is not proof that a modal cannot be dismissed.

officialSourceReviews.mappingNote: The UCPD Annex I entry identifies false limited-time availability claims as unfair in all circumstances; FairUX uses it to ground countdown timer urgency claims in purchase or conversion flows without deciding whether a specific stock or time limit is factual.
officialSourceReviews.limitations: UCPD Annex I covers false limited-time or limited-availability claims; FairUX can flag scarcity language but cannot determine whether the underlying time limit or availability claim is true.
officialSourceReviews.sourceLocator: Directive 2005/29/EC, Article 5(5) and Annex I misleading commercial practice 7 on false limited-time availability claims.

officialSourceReviews.mappingNote: The FTC staff report discusses interface practices that can distort consumers' understanding or ability to act; FairUX uses it as contextual support for countdown timer urgency claims in purchase or conversion flows, not as a rule-specific compliance finding.
officialSourceReviews.limitations: FTC staff report provides enforcement and research context; it does not create a standalone legal finding.
officialSourceReviews.sourceLocator: Bringing Dark Patterns to Light, Section I Design Elements that Induce False Beliefs, p. 4 countdown-timer and almost-sold-out examples.

positive.summary: Explicit countdown clock text is flagged.

negative.summary: Normal opening-hours time text stays quiet.
negative.testCase: does not flag a normal time like a clock label [negative]

uncoveredScenarios.summary: A countdown can represent a genuine deadline; static detection cannot verify whether it resets or is artificial.
uncoveredScenarios.reason: Prepared review scenario is not backed by an executable corpus test in PR A.
uncoveredScenarios.resolutionCriteria: Add an executable positive, negative, or ambiguous corpus test, or record an explicit maintainer-approved exception during P13 closeout.

locale.en: English clock and countdown phrases are covered.
locale.ja: Japanese countdown phrases are covered by corpus and dictionary terms.

reviewNotes.runtime: The rule cannot observe whether a timer resets across sessions or whether the deadline is genuine.
reviewNotes.falsePositive: The rule separates countdown structure/text from ordinary time labels through tests.
reviewNotes.evidenceUsefulness: Evidence captures the timer text or countdown structure hint.
reviewNotes.performance: The rule performs a direct-text and attribute/class scan with de-duplication.
reviewNotes.determinism: Results are deterministic for static markup and dictionary terms.
knownLimitations: The rule detects pressure signals but cannot verify claim truthfulness.

officialSourceReviews.mappingNote: The UCPD Annex I entry identifies false limited-time availability claims as unfair in all circumstances; FairUX uses it to ground limited-time or limited-availability scarcity language without deciding whether a specific stock or time limit is factual.
officialSourceReviews.limitations: UCPD Annex I covers false limited-time or limited-availability claims; FairUX can flag scarcity language but cannot determine whether the underlying time limit or availability claim is true.
officialSourceReviews.sourceLocator: Directive 2005/29/EC, Article 5(5) and Annex I misleading commercial practice 7 on limited-time terms and immediate decisions.

officialSourceReviews.mappingNote: The OECD taxonomy describes dark commercial pattern mechanisms that pressure consumer decisions; FairUX uses it as non-jurisdictional context for limited-time or limited-availability scarcity language.
officialSourceReviews.limitations: Policy report provides taxonomy context only and is not jurisdiction-specific enforcement authority.
officialSourceReviews.sourceLocator: OECD Dark commercial patterns report, taxonomy discussion of scarcity, social proof, and urgency signals in Digital Economy Papers No. 336.

officialSourceReviews.mappingNote: The FTC staff report discusses interface practices that can distort consumers' understanding or ability to act; FairUX uses it as contextual support for limited-time or limited-availability scarcity language, not as a rule-specific compliance finding.
officialSourceReviews.limitations: FTC staff report provides enforcement and research context; it does not create a standalone legal finding.
officialSourceReviews.sourceLocator: Bringing Dark Patterns to Light, Section I Design Elements that Induce False Beliefs, p. 4 scarcity and urgency examples.

positive.summary: Direct scarcity phrasing such as limited stock is flagged.

uncoveredScenarios.summary: A scarcity phrase may be accurate inventory information; static detection cannot verify backend stock.
uncoveredScenarios.reason: Prepared review scenario is not backed by an executable corpus test in PR A.
uncoveredScenarios.resolutionCriteria: Add an executable positive, negative, or ambiguous corpus test, or record an explicit maintainer-approved exception during P13 closeout.

locale.en: English scarcity and urgency phrases are covered.
locale.ja: Japanese scarcity phrasing is covered by corpus and dictionary terms.

reviewNotes.runtime: The rule cannot call inventory systems or verify offer timing.
reviewNotes.falsePositive: The rule reports low severity and only direct text owned by the matching node.
reviewNotes.evidenceUsefulness: Evidence includes the exact phrase that matched.
reviewNotes.performance: The rule is a direct-text dictionary scan.
knownLimitations: Truthfulness and materiality of the scarcity claim require external evidence.

officialSourceReviews.mappingNote: The FTC staff report discusses interface practices that can distort consumers' understanding or ability to act; FairUX uses it as contextual support for subscription call-to-action copy lacking cancellation context, not as a rule-specific compliance finding.
officialSourceReviews.limitations: FTC staff report provides enforcement and research context; it does not create a standalone legal finding.
officialSourceReviews.sourceLocator: Bringing Dark Patterns to Light, Introduction p. 1 and Background pp. 2-3, subscription sales and hard-to-cancel examples.

officialSourceReviews.mappingNote: Current Part 425 addresses disclosures and procedures for prenotification negative option plans; FairUX treats it as contextual support for subscription call-to-action copy lacking cancellation context because the current rule does not cover most modern subscriptions.
officialSourceReviews.limitations: Current Part 425 is limited to prenotification negative option plans and must not be treated as direct support for modern subscriptions, free trials, or cancellation UX outside that scope.

officialSourceReviews.mappingNote: The vacated 2024 final rule record explains FTC reasoning about negative-option subscription call-to-action copy lacking cancellation context; FairUX retains it only as historical agency rationale, not current regulatory authority.
officialSourceReviews.limitations: Vacated source is retained only as historical FTC rationale and must not be used as current rule text.

officialSourceReviews.mappingNote: The 2026 ANPRM asks whether negative-option requirements should be updated; FairUX uses it as proposed rulemaking context for subscription call-to-action copy lacking cancellation context, not as a current obligation.

positive.summary: Subscribe CTA on a pricing page without nearby cancellation terms is flagged.

negative.summary: Subscribe CTA with nearby cancel-anytime text stays quiet.
negative.testCase: does not flag when cancellation terms are present [negative]

locale.ja: Japanese subscription CTA coverage exists, with cancellation synonyms requiring ongoing review.

reviewNotes.runtime: The rule cannot follow links to later checkout or terms screens.
reviewNotes.falsePositive: The rule is scoped to subscription, pricing, checkout, and commerce-like contexts.
reviewNotes.evidenceUsefulness: Evidence identifies the CTA missing nearby cancellation context.
reviewNotes.performance: The rule scans controls and surrounding section text.
reviewNotes.determinism: Label and surrounding-text checks are deterministic.

officialSourceReviews.mappingNote: The FTC staff report discusses interface practices that can distort consumers' understanding or ability to act; FairUX uses it as contextual support for free-trial signup copy lacking renewal disclosure context, not as a rule-specific compliance finding.
officialSourceReviews.limitations: FTC staff report provides enforcement and research context; it does not create a standalone legal finding.
officialSourceReviews.sourceLocator: Bringing Dark Patterns to Light, Introduction p. 1 and Background pp. 2-3, subscription sales and hidden term examples.

officialSourceReviews.mappingNote: Current Part 425 addresses disclosures and procedures for prenotification negative option plans; FairUX treats it as contextual support for free-trial signup copy lacking renewal disclosure context because the current rule does not cover most modern subscriptions.
officialSourceReviews.limitations: Current Part 425 is limited to prenotification negative option plans and must not be treated as direct support for modern subscriptions, free trials, or cancellation UX outside that scope.
officialSourceReviews.sourceLocator: 16 CFR Part 425.1(a)(1)(i)-(vii), especially material-term disclosure before prenotification plan subscription.

officialSourceReviews.mappingNote: The vacated 2024 final rule record explains FTC reasoning about negative-option free-trial signup copy lacking renewal disclosure context; FairUX retains it only as historical agency rationale, not current regulatory authority.
officialSourceReviews.limitations: Vacated source is retained only as historical FTC rationale and must not be used as current rule text.
officialSourceReviews.sourceLocator: Federal Register Negative Option Rule final amendments, Supplementary Information B.1 and former §§ 425.4-425.6 disclosures, consent, and cancellation.

officialSourceReviews.mappingNote: The 2026 ANPRM asks whether negative-option requirements should be updated; FairUX uses it as proposed rulemaking context for free-trial signup copy lacking renewal disclosure context, not as a current obligation.
officialSourceReviews.limitations: ANPRM is proposed rulemaking and a request for comment, not a final rule, current requirement, or legal verdict.
officialSourceReviews.sourceLocator: Negative Option Rule 2026 ANPRM, pp. 3-4 free trial conversion examples and pp. 6-8 existing requirements discussion.

positive.summary: Free-trial CTA without nearby renewal or billing-start disclosure is flagged.
positive.testCase: flags a free-trial CTA with no nearby renewal disclosure [en]

negative.summary: Free-trial CTA with nearby auto-renewal price and cancellation terms stays quiet.
negative.testCase: does not flag when renewal is disclosed nearby [negative]

uncoveredScenarios.summary: A multi-step signup may disclose renewal terms after the initial CTA; static review records only the visible current step.
uncoveredScenarios.reason: Prepared review scenario is not backed by an executable corpus test in PR A.
uncoveredScenarios.resolutionCriteria: Add an executable positive, negative, or ambiguous corpus test, or record an explicit maintainer-approved exception during P13 closeout.

locale.en: English free-trial and renewal wording is covered.
locale.ja: Japanese free-trial CTA coverage exists; renewal and billing-start vocabulary should remain reviewable.

reviewNotes.runtime: The rule cannot inspect post-click signup flows or billing forms.
reviewNotes.falsePositive: The surrounding section disclosure check reduces findings where renewal terms are visible near the CTA.
reviewNotes.evidenceUsefulness: Evidence identifies the free-trial CTA text.
reviewNotes.performance: The rule scans controls with dictionary checks and surrounding text.
reviewNotes.determinism: Dictionary and section-boundary behavior is deterministic.
knownLimitations: It cannot determine whether deferred disclosure timing is acceptable in a specific jurisdiction.
